rationales.power: He is angry that Kramer presented the results "like they were his," showing strong concern with recognition, influence, and whose name gets attached to success.
rationales.conformity: He stayed quiet because "picking a fight on camera would only hurt me," deliberately restraining himself to avoid conflict and reputational fallout.

rationales.hedonism: He says Lotte opened a special bottle and he "couldn't even enjoy it properly" because he was fixated on the slight, putting pleasure aside.
rationales.achievement: He frames six years of building the region and wanting his name attached to the outcome as proof of competence and career progress.
rationales.power: He explicitly links public credit to advancement: "It's how you move up" and how people think of him for "the next VP seat."

rationales.hedonism: He canceled dinner at "that new place in Katendrecht" because work left him too exhausted to enjoy it.
rationales.achievement: When two leads called in sick, he spent "six hours on the floor sorting parcels," prioritizing operational performance despite the physical cost.

rationales.hedonism: Even on a "Saturday sailing trip" he was checking emails and says he "barely noticed the water," showing work crowding out enjoyment.
rationales.power: His frustration centers on being sidelined by Kramer and on what it means to be the person who "run[s] things," reflecting an ongoing focus on status and control.
rationales.conformity: He says Kramer was condescending but he "smiled through anyway" and raised the issue in a "measured" way, carefully managing himself to avoid open friction.

rationales.achievement: Securing approval to hire for the Antwerp hub reflects concrete progress on a major work objective and expansion responsibility.
rationales.power: He celebrates that it is "my budget, my picks, no committee" and says it feels like "actual authority," directly emphasizing control over decisions.

rationales.hedonism: He calls the evening with "good food, the right people, candles on the canal" and Lotte in her element "the kind of evening I live for," clearly embracing sensory pleasure.
rationales.power: He notes the guests' industry influence and says he knows "exactly what those relationships are worth," treating the dinner as a way to build status and leverage.

rationales.hedonism: He gave up a Bruges weekend with a "rooftop terrace" and "Michelin-star restaurant" to work, then realized he could have been there instead of "eating cheese toast."
rationales.achievement: He keeps pushing the permit problem because he "can't afford" delay with the VP shortlist coming, putting career progress ahead of rest.
rationales.benevolence: He chose work over time with "the person I like most in the world," despite seeing that the extra Saturday effort changed little and left Lotte going without him.

rationales.hedonism: He deliberately took Friday off, put his "phone in a drawer," then sailed, grilled fish, drank Riesling, and watched the light change on the water.
rationales.power: He overrode the site manager's layout himself because his plan would create a bottleneck, showing a strong impulse to take control and impose his judgment.

rationales.hedonism: The possible move would mean "more time in transit" and "fewer evenings on the terrace," with enjoyment and domestic pleasures treated as likely sacrifices.
rationales.power: He is pulled by "more status, more scope, more say in how the company runs" and describes the role as what he has been "manoeuvring towards for years."

rationales.self_direction: He values that "nobody tells me when to start, when to stop" and rejects the idea that benefits and a standard path are the only real options.
rationales.stimulation: He contrasts waiting for "some dude to get his Thai food" with rappelling into a canyon fire and thinking "this is what life should feel like," suggesting his current routine falls short of the intensity he craves.
rationales.achievement: He tracks concrete progress, citing "$800 in sales this month" for the drone business and calling it "momentum."
rationales.security: He explicitly resists "a real job with benefits, a retirement plan, maybe a ring," dismissing stability as the goal.

rationales.self_direction: He insists he is not taking "some cubicle job" to satisfy other people and defends building a path others do not recognize.
rationales.achievement: He lays out a concrete benchmark: if the drone business hits "$2000/month by December," that covers rent and creates runway toward full-time work.
rationales.security: He rejects Kevin's stability-focused argument about a 401k and conventional long-term safety, favoring a riskier self-built path.
rationales.conformity: He pushes back against social pressure from Rachel's brother and refuses to reshape his life just so others can stop worrying about him.

rationales.self_direction: He reaches out to Dario and resonates with "stop explaining yourself to people who already decided you're wrong," reaffirming his own unconventional path.
rationales.conformity: The whole entry resists others' approval standards, especially the pressure to justify himself to people who have already judged him.

rationales.self_direction: He says being asked for "stability" feels like being asked to "become someone else," strongly defending the right to live on his own terms.
rationales.achievement: He keeps framing the drone work as "building toward something" and would "rather be broke and building something" than settle for comfort.
rationales.security: He openly rejects the stable path Rachel seems to want, saying his parents had "stable jobs and they were miserable" and preferring uncertainty.
rationales.benevolence: Rachel asks "Where am I in it?" and he admits he has been focused on proving "my way works" without thinking enough about whether she wanted to be part of that ride.

rationales.stimulation: He says driving is "just time traded for money" and that the drone work "doesn't hit the same," describing a life that feels flat rather than energizing.
rationales.achievement: He no longer feels his current work matters and says he is "not sure" the bridge leads anywhere, signaling a loss of confidence in effective progress.

rationales.conformity: He swallows his pride and says yes to counseling, recognizing that the old instinct to treat help as weakness is something he needs to restrain.
rationales.benevolence: He accepts that Rachel "deserves to know the stakes" and that if he succeeds or fails, "she's there," showing real concern for the relationship rather than only his own project.

rationales.stimulation: He describes himself as "wired to move" and fears stillness so strongly that it feels like "dying," showing a deep drive toward motion and intensity.
rationales.achievement: He says happiness has usually been tied to being "good at it," doing something "hard," or proving "the concept worked," linking fulfillment to striving and accomplishment.
rationales.security: He is unsettled by the idea of not working, by injury, and by his father's collapse after retirement; stillness and stability feel threatening rather than comforting.
rationales.benevolence: He keeps returning to what this means for Rachel and says he wants "the right kind of motion" to be "something that includes her," showing care for preserving the relationship.
